opp.name: ACLogistics_CLM_Intake
opp.next_step: 50% confidence 29th May- good overview/1st meeting with Cody (Christian) CIO. He was interested in how to future proof the business- as they are young and recently "built" by Ayala group. (mentioned a current CLM project being evaluated (we mentioned GCash did the same) Will be looking for a demo to include Matilde (CPO) next and getting NDA signed off BD update Start the NDA process. opp.ais_why: Ayala Group are pushing for ways to increase AI value

metrics.narrative: The buyer has articulated qualitative business pain but has not yet quantified savings, cost reduction, or ROI targets. Christian (Cody) Dy stated on the 2026-05-29 call that AC Logistics is 'fairly young' (formed 2022, acquired Air21 and 5 other firms, now 8-9 business units) and framed the need as future-proofing and scaling procurement operations, saying 'we're not coming in from a problem, but I don't want it to be a problem eventually.' He emphasized that 'procurement's pretty key to logistics' (sourcing truckers, warehouses, system solutions, raw materials) and that current processes are 'basic' and need scale. Pain points include lack of structured procurement process, user experience challenges with legacy ERP (SAP mentioned as difficult UX: 'SAP or any kind of ERP has always been a challenge with that... that has always been my pet peeve'), and the need for conversational, easy-to-use systems for warehouse users who 'might not be totally digitally savvy.' No quantified impact (cycle time, cost per PO, compliance gaps, savings targets) has been stated. The recommended move is to co-develop a quantified value case during the demo and ROI workshop, tying CLM and intake management to measurable logistics procurement outcomes (contract cycle time, tail-spend savings via agentic AI, supplier onboarding speed).

champion.narrative: No champion has been secured. Christian (Cody) Dy, CIO and Decision Maker contact role, is engaged and enthusiastic (he drove the 2026-05-29 discovery call, asked detailed questions about UX, AI, pricing, ERP integration, and logistics customers, and stated 'I'd love to hear more about Zycus... let's schedule it'), but there is no evidence yet of active advocacy, internal selling, or access to the Economic Buyer. Cody controls the technical and business-process evaluation and has the authority to advance the deal (he confirmed 'CLM part is likely to happen this year... it's gonna happen this year'), but he has not yet introduced Zycus to senior leadership (President Irma Diaz-Guevara or iALA corporate sponsors), shared internal docs, or pushed back on competitors. He mentioned that the CPO Matilde Villaluz 'already met you guys already' and 'immediately replied' during the call, suggesting prior Zycus engagement and coordination, but Matilde has not yet joined a call or demonstrated advocacy. Cody's enthusiasm is a strong foundation for champion development (he explicitly said 'let's move forward with that conversation... we would definitely love to have more conversation'), and his role as CIO gives him influence over systems selection, but he is not yet actively selling Zycus internally or navigating political barriers. Recommended move: during the next demo with the CPO, co-develop the business case with Cody and Matilde, test their willingness to introduce Zycus to the EB and President, and explicitly recruit Cody as champion by giving him the ROI narrative, competitive differentiation (vs Coupa/Ariba), and iALA peer references (gCash, SM Group) to evangelize internally.

sources: Avoma call 2026-05-29, Christian (Cody) Dy questions re SAP/Oracle competition
SF Next_Step__c 2026-05-29 'currently looking for a CLM'
competition.narrative: Competition is confirmed from two authoritative sources: Salesforce Competitors__c field lists 'Coupa;SAP Ariba,' and the broader competitive landscape is visible in the buyer's evaluation posture. No competitor was explicitly named or discussed on the 2026-05-29 call, but the buyer's questions and context reveal the competitive frame. Christian (Cody) Dy asked 'most of your modules are in competition with SAP and Oracle... is that where Zycus is going for, or can it complement some existing?' and confirmed they have SAP ERP, indicating SAP Ariba is a natural incumbent consideration. He also stated 'we're currently looking for a CLM already' (Next Step 2026-05-29), signaling an active procurement cycle where Coupa and other CLM/source-to-contract vendors are likely being evaluated in parallel. Carl (Zycus) acknowledged the SAP Ariba landscape ('SAP almost single-handedly bought The Philippines... a lot of Ariba procurement stuff around... unfortunately didn't get the same levels of investment... it's outdated, not interoperable') and positioned Zycus as the modern replacement. No competitor has been ruled out, shortlisted, or directly compared. The wedge: Zycus wins on embedded agentic AI (Merlin), conversational intake via the relevant stakeholder (critical for the buyer's non-technical warehouse users), deep source-to-contract functionality (vs Ariba's stale modules), and strong iALA/Philippines presence (gCash, SM Group, BDO references). Coupa is a pricing and UX competitor; SAP Ariba is the legacy/status-quo threat. Recommended move: during the next demo, explicitly position Zycus against Coupa (ease of use, AI depth, modular pricing flexibility for a young scaling business) and SAP Ariba (modern platform, superior UX, no ERP lock-in), and provide iALA peer references to neutralize competitive FUD.

identify_pain.narrative: The buyer has clearly articulated operational pain tied to their young, rapidly scaling logistics business. Christian (Cody) Dy stated on the 2026-05-29 call that AC Logistics is 'fairly young' (iALA launched iCLogix in 2022, acquired Air21 and 5 other logistics firms, now 8-9 business units, and closed a joint venture with the relevant stakeholder early 2026) and 'we're not coming in from a problem, but I don't want it to be a problem eventually... part of being a young company allows us to have good opportunities to see what's out there.' He named procurement as foundational to logistics operations: 'procurement's pretty key to logistics... sourcing for truckers, warehouses, system solutions, raw materials... everything procurement.' He described current processes as 'basic' and unable to scale: 'process is there, but I'd say it's basic... in order for the organization to scale, we would need to have these kinds of solutions.' Pain owner: Cody (CIO, technical/systems owner) and Matilde Villaluz (CPO, procurement process owner). Specific pain points confirmed: (1) poor user experience in legacy ERP systems ('SAP or any kind of ERP has always been a challenge... that has always been my pet peeve'), critical because warehouse users are 'not totally digitally savvy'; (2) lack of future-proofing and structured procurement to support rapid M&A integration and growth ('he was interested in how to future proof the business' per Next Step); (3) need for CLM to manage contracts at scale ('we're currently looking for a CLM already'). Cody explicitly framed this as proactive prevention ('I don't want it to be a problem eventually') rather than crisis-driven, which positions Zycus as a strategic enabler for scale. The pain is confirmed and owned; the gap is quantification (no cycle-time, cost, or risk metric yet tied to the pain).

paper_process.narrative: The paper process is partially visible: NDA is the immediate next step, but MSA, SOW, legal review, InfoSec, and procurement channel have not been discussed. The Next Step 2026-05-29 states 'Start the NDA process' and 'Zycus to reconnect over email for due diligence and NDA formalities,' and on the call Carl said 'We need an NDA, did you say? We'll share our NDA.' This indicates NDA is in motion as of late May 2026. No contracting mechanics (MSA template, order form, SOW scope, legal redline cycle, InfoSec questionnaire, data-privacy review) have been raised. No partner or SI involvement has been mentioned. The account is part of iALA, a large conglomerate, which may impose corporate legal, procurement, or InfoSec gates, but this has not been confirmed. The buyer has SAP ERP and is evaluating integration, so an ERP/systems integration scoping session will be required (covered in the demo plan per Next Step: 'align the next conversation around CLM, source-to-contract, AI, and pricing context'). Recommended move: after NDA execution, surface the full paper path during the demo with the CPO (does iALA corporate legal review all SaaS contracts? Is there a standard MSA? What is the InfoSec review cycle? Is procurement centralized or business-unit-led?), and establish a realistic legal-to-signature timeline within the 2026-12-29 close window.

sources: SF OpportunityContactRole (Christian Dy = Decision Maker, Matilde Villaluz = Evaluator)
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer has been identified or engaged. The discovery call 2026-05-29 involved Christian (Cody) Dy, the CIO and Decision Maker contact role, who controls technical and business-process decisions but did not discuss budget authority, pricing approval, or commercial sign-off. Matilde Villaluz, the Chief Procurement Officer (Evaluator contact role), was mentioned as the next meeting participant and 'immediately replied' via email during the call, indicating engagement, but has not yet joined a buyer-facing session. No CFO, President, or C-level sponsor appeared on any call. The Next Step and call notes reference scheduling a demo 'with the Chief Procurement Officer' and exploring 'indicative costing details' but do not name who will approve the commercial. Cody asked about pricing models ('how does the pricing model run for Zycus? Do you have is it credits based per license?') on 2026-05-29, a commercial curiosity signal, but no authority figure has been introduced. The recommended move is to map budget authority during the next demo session (who approves $150k SaaS spend, whether iALA corporate or AC Logistics unit holds the purse, and whether Matilde or a senior exec sponsors the commercial), and to secure an executive sponsor (President Irma Diaz-Guevara or iALA corporate finance) before Vendor Selected stage.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is emerging but not yet formalized. The 2026-05-29 call sequence shows CIO-led discovery (Christian Cody Dy, Decision Maker contact role) as the entry point, with the Chief Procurement Officer (Matilde Villaluz, Evaluator contact role) joining next. Cody confirmed on the call that Matilde 'already met you guys already... she immediately replied' via email, indicating prior Zycus engagement at iALA corporate or another business unit and active coordination. The Next Step 2026-05-29 outlines a three-step near-term process: (1) NDA execution, (2) demo with CPO included, (3) face-to-face meeting in Manila 'when Akshay and Carl are available.' Cody stated 'we're currently looking for a CLM already' and 'it's gonna happen this year,' signaling a live procurement cycle with a 2026 target. No formal RFP, shortlist, or approval-chain detail has been shared. Cody asked about pricing models, a signal that commercial evaluation is on his radar, but no procurement/legal/InfoSec gate or multi-stakeholder approval process has been named. The account is part of the iALA conglomerate (AC Logistics formed 2022, acquired Air21 and 5+ firms, joint venture with the relevant stakeholder in 2026), suggesting potential corporate oversight or shared services approval, but this has not been confirmed. Recommended move: during the next demo, map the full approval chain (does iALA corporate finance or legal review the contract? Is there a sourcing committee? Who signs the SOW?) and establish a mutual close plan with milestones (demo, BRD fit, pricing, EB sign-off, legal, close by 2026-12-29).

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer has stated high-level evaluation priorities but has not yet documented formal decision criteria, ShoeFit/BRD requirements, or a scored RFP. On the 2026-05-29 call, Christian (Cody) Dy emphasized three must-haves: (1) user experience that is 'conversational' and accessible to non-technical warehouse users ('users who are warehouse people who might not be totally digitally savvy... the user experience for our users' is 'very important to me in my role'); (2) integration with existing SAP ERP without replacing it ('we do have SAP... what's the benefit... source to contract within the same ecosystem of Zycus?'); and (3) embedded AI and future-proofing ('he was interested in how to future proof the business' per Next Step). He explicitly called out poor UX in legacy ERP systems ('SAP or any kind of ERP has always been a challenge... that has always been my pet peeve') and asked whether Zycus can complement rather than compete with the relevant stakeholder demo script from the call highlighted AI (Merlin agentic negotiation, intake via the relevant stakeholder), ERP integration (SAP/Oracle), and modular source-to-contract functionality, and Cody responded positively ('I like those things you pointed out... I'd love to hear more about the sourcing to contract'). No formal scorecard, RFP criteria, or written BRD has been shared. The next demo with the CPO will surface procurement-specific criteria (supplier onboarding, contract obligation management, compliance guardrails, AI ROI). Recommended move: co-author a decision criteria matrix during the demo and validate it in writing with both Cody (technical/UX fit) and the CPO (procurement process fit).

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI appetite is confirmed but not yet quantified via AIS field (no AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, or AIS_Why__c in Salesforce). Buyer stated interest in 'how to future proof the business' (Next Step 2026-05-29) and asked about AI pricing models on 2026-05-29 call, signaling active evaluation. Demo highlighted Merlin agentic AI and conversational intake, and Cody responded positively. Buyer context (young, scaling logistics business needing easy UX for non-technical warehouse users) fits AI-first platform. Inferred tier: AI Curious (genuine interest, no quantified AI ROI case yet co-developed). Recommended move: make Merlin the centerpiece of the next demo and co-develop a quantified AI ROI case (tail-spend savings, contract cycle time, intake adoption) to elevate appetite to AI Hungry.

items.detail: No Economic Buyer identified or engaged; budget authority unmapped. CIO and CPO are engaged but no CFO, President, or C-level sponsor has appeared. Risk: deal advances to late-stage without commercial sign-off and stalls at pricing/approval gate.

Buyer asked about AI pricing models ('is it credits based per license? What's the how does the model work?') on 2026-05-29 call but no indicative pricing has been shared. Carl said 'I couldn't give you a price without getting a little bit more context from you' and committed to 'put together something indicative around costing.' Risk: pricing misalignment or sticker shock if not addressed early.

Parallel CLM evaluation in progress ('we're currently looking for a CLM already' per Next Step 2026-05-29); Coupa and SAP Ariba are named competitors but not yet discussed or neutralized on calls. Risk: buyer runs parallel demos with Coupa/Ariba and competitive FUD is not countered early.

champion_strength.summary: Champion is developing. CIO Christian (Cody) Dy is engaged, enthusiastic, and driving the evaluation (he initiated the discovery call, confirmed 2026 CLM timeline, and committed to next steps including demo with CPO), but he has not yet demonstrated active internal advocacy, introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer or senior leadership, or navigated political barriers. His role as Decision Maker and CIO gives him influence over systems selection, and his stated priorities (UX, AI, ERP integration) align strongly with Zycus positioning. CPO Matilde Villaluz has prior Zycus engagement (per Cody: 'she already met you guys') and is coordinating actively, but has not yet joined a call on THIS deal. Recommended move: co-develop the quantified ROI case with Cody and Matilde during the demo, give them the competitive differentiation narrative (vs Coupa/Ariba) and iALA peer references, and explicitly recruit Cody as champion by testing his willingness to introduce Zycus to the EB and evangelize internally.

Strong product fit: Merlin AI, conversational intake via Microsoft Teams, ERP integration, and logistics customer references (SIRVA, Bidvest) align with buyer priorities stated on call
Concrete next steps logged: NDA, demo with CPO, F2F Manila meeting, indicative pricing (per Next Step 2026-05-29 and call commitments)
Recent engagement: LastActivityDate 2026-06-14 (12 days ago); no cooling signals
confidence_signals.summary: Positive early-stage momentum: engaged Decision Maker (CIO Christian Cody Dy drove discovery call, confirmed 2026 CLM timeline, committed to demo with CPO), clear pain articulated (scaling logistics procurement, poor ERP UX, future-proofing), live CLM procurement cycle, strong product fit (Merlin AI, Teams intake, ERP integration align with buyer priorities), and concrete next steps logged (NDA, demo, F2F Manila meeting). No cooling signals; LastActivityDate 2026-06-14 (12 days ago) shows recent engagement. Vulnerabilities: no Economic Buyer mapped, no champion yet secured, no competitive positioning executed, and no quantified ROI case co-developed. Overall: healthy early-stage deal with actionable next steps and credible 2026-12-29 close window.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 2026-05-29 (28 days ago). Stage Qualified with 186 days to close (2026-12-29). Discovery call 2026-05-29 established buyer interest in CLM plus broader source-to-contract. Next Step logged 2026-05-29 plans NDA, demo with the Chief Procurement Officer, and F2F meeting. LastActivityDate 2026-06-14 (12 days ago) shows recent engagement. Time-in-stage 28 days is appropriate for Qualified in a 12-15 month enterprise cycle. No slip history observed. Pacing is consistent with early discovery milestone.

north_star_verdict.headline: Early-stage CLM deal with strong discovery and Decision Maker engagement showing initial stall signal: 12 days silent post-activity with no NDA, demo, or CPO meeting completed despite concrete next steps logged May 29th.

best_practice_check.summary: Strong early-stage fundamentals (engaged Decision Maker, clear pain, live CLM procurement, credible timeline), but three levers will raise win probability: (1) recruit the CIO as champion and co-develop the quantified ROI case with both CIO and CPO during the demo to create internal evangelists; (2) map budget authority and secure Economic Buyer sponsorship before Shortlisted stage (who approves $150k SaaS at AC Logistics or iALA corporate?); (3) explicitly position Zycus against Coupa (AI depth, UX, iALA references) and SAP Ariba (modern platform, no ERP lock-in) during the demo to neutralize competitive FUD early.

competitive_position.summary: Coupa and SAP Ariba are the named competitors per SF Competitors__c field, with SAP Ariba as the incumbent/legacy threat (buyer has SAP ERP) and Coupa as the modern peer alternative. No competitor has been discussed, shortlisted, or ruled out on calls as of 2026-05-29. The buyer is in active CLM procurement ('we're currently looking for a CLM already'), so parallel evaluation is assumed. Zycus is well-positioned on embedded agentic AI (Merlin), conversational UX via Microsoft Teams (critical for non-technical warehouse users), and strong iALA/Philippines presence (gCash, SM Group, BDO references). The single strongest current threat is Coupa (modern, name-brand CLM peer), followed by SAP Ariba inertia (legacy ERP lock-in). Wedge: Zycus offers superior AI depth, user experience for logistics/warehouse users, modular pricing flexibility for a young scaling business, and deep iALA penetration that Coupa cannot match.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is strong and resonating. The buyer explicitly stated interest in 'how to future proof the business' (Next Step 2026-05-29) and Cody asked detailed questions about AI pricing models ('how does the pricing model run for Zycus? Do you have is it credits based per license?') on the 2026-05-29 call, signaling genuine curiosity and evaluation intent. The Zycus demo highlighted Merlin agentic AI (autonomous negotiating agent Anna, 34% tail-spend savings on auctions), conversational intake via Microsoft Teams, and embedded AI across source-to-contract modules, and Cody responded positively ('I like those things you pointed out... I'd love to hear more'). Products field includes 'Merlin Intake,' confirming AI scope in the commercial. No AIS score/status in Salesforce, so appetite is inferred from call dialogue, not quantified. The buyer's context (young, scaling logistics business with 8-9 acquired units, SAP ERP legacy, warehouse users needing easy UX) is a natural fit for Merlin intake and agentic procurement. Positioning is on track; recommended move is to make Merlin the centerpiece of the next demo (live intake via Teams, agentic negotiation use case for logistics tail-spend like trucking/warehouse sourcing) and tie AI ROI to quantified logistics procurement outcomes.

items.quote: Cody stated 'the user experience for our users is very important to me in my role... you would have users who are warehouse people who might not be totally digitally savvy' and called out 'SAP or any kind of ERP has always been a challenge... that has always been my pet peeve' on 2026-05-29 call; Zycus demo showed Merlin Intake via Microsoft Teams with natural-language conversational interface
items.criterion: User experience: conversational, accessible to non-technical warehouse users

items.quote: Cody asked 'what's the benefit... source to contract within the same ecosystem of Zycus... we do have SAP' on 2026-05-29 call; Akshay and Carl confirmed 'we integrate very closely with SAP and Oracle... 95% of customers have Oracle or SAP... we don't compete with ERP systems' and demoed source-to-contract modules that layer on top of ERP
items.source: Avoma call 2026-05-29, Christian (Cody) Dy, Akshay, Carl Kimball
items.criterion: ERP integration: complement SAP without replacing it

items.quote: Next Step 2026-05-29 states 'He was interested in how to future proof the business- as they are young and recently built by Ayala group'; Cody said 'in order for the organization to scale, we would need to have these kinds of solutions' on call; Zycus demo highlighted Merlin agentic AI (autonomous negotiating agent Anna, up to 34% tail-spend savings) and conversational intake
items.source: Avoma call 2026-05-29, Christian (Cody) Dy; SF Next_Step__c 2026-05-29

items.quote: Cody asked 'do you have any clients who are in the logistics space that are already using Zycus?' on 2026-05-29 call; Akshay cited SIRVA and Bidvest Logistics (Africa's biggest logistics company, using full source-to-pay for trucks, warehouses, forklifts, shipment file integration with Cargowise); Carl committed to 'table getting back to you with some of our logistics customers with the names of them and maybe a little fact sheet'
items.criterion: Logistics industry fit and peer references

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong early fit on buyer's stated priorities: conversational UX for non-technical users (Merlin Intake via Microsoft Teams directly addresses Cody's 'pet peeve' with SAP UX), embedded AI for future-proofing (Merlin agentic platform aligns with Cody's 'how to future proof the business' need), and ERP integration without replacement (Zycus source-to-contract complements SAP ERP per demo on 2026-05-29 call). Gaps: no formal BRD or ShoeFit criteria yet documented, and no quantified ROI case co-developed. Fit is inferred from discovery dialogue, not validated against written requirements.

hard.ais_why: Ayala Group are pushing for ways to increase AI value

value.how_we_win: Embed agentic AI (Merlin autonomous negotiation, 34% tail-spend savings) and conversational intake via Microsoft Teams as the differentiated UX for non-technical warehouse users; leverage iALA peer references (gCash, SM Group) and modular pricing flexibility for a young, scaling logistics business; position Coupa as less AI-mature and harder to deploy at scale

packets.subject: Coupa

value.name: SAP Ariba
value.quote: Buyer asked 'most of your modules are in competition with SAP and Oracle... is that where Zycus is going for, or can it complement?' and confirmed SAP ERP in place; Carl positioned Ariba as 'outdated, not interoperable... didn't get the same levels of investment' on 2026-05-29 call
value.source: SF Competitors__c field; Avoma call 2026-05-29, Christian (Cody) Dy and Carl Kimball
value.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the modern, non-ERP-locked replacement for stale Ariba modules; emphasize superior UX (Cody's stated pet peeve: 'SAP or any kind of ERP has always been a challenge... that has always been my pet peeve'), seamless SAP ERP integration without vendor lock-in, and deep source-to-contract functionality that Ariba no longer invests in; use Philippines market momentum (SM, BDO, gCash all moved off Ariba-class legacy to Zycus) as proof

packets.subject: SAP Ariba
